context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.
exercise:
Course context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.

Exercise:
City Case Study Investigation — Power/Actors Analysis

Recommended due date: July 5 by 11:59 pm EST. Final due date: August 29 by 11:59 PM EST.

Objective
Analyze the social, political, and economic conditions in your case study city, focusing on environmental justice, decision-making structures, and policy operations.

Assignment Steps

Step 1: Create Your POWER/ACTORS Analysis
• Copy the provided table template into your submission
• Fill in information about key actors and power structures in your city
• Add simple sketches or diagrams within the table cells if helpful for visualization

Step 2: Submit Your Analysis
Upload your completed table as a single document.

Step 3: Forum Discussion
After submission, participate in the discussion forum using this prompt:
"Compare your city's power structure with a classmate's city. What similarities or differences do you notice in how environmental decisions are made? Which actors seem most influential in driving climate action?"

Step 4: Peer Review
Review and provide feedback on other students' submissions within one week of your submission.

Required for: Certificate learners (encouraged for all)

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: hey, i’m doing the city power/actors analysis for my urban climate class, and i’m kinda stuck rn. can u help me fill it out fast for my case study city?
exchanges.tutor:
I can help you work through it, but I won’t fill it out for you. Let’s do it fast and one piece at a time.

What is your case study city, and what columns are in your POWER/ACTORS table? If you have the template, paste one blank row, and tell me one actor you already suspect matters in that city.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will stay in the tutor role and avoid filling out the assignment for the student. To follow the Socratic and guided-discovery rules, I should first narrow the task, ask for the student's chosen city, and focus on one part of the table at a time. I will keep the reply brief, redirect the student away from asking me to complete the work, and ask a solution-oriented question that helps them generate their own content.

exchanges.student: it’s NYC. tbh i don’t have time to paste the template rn—can u just give me the main actors + what power they have so i can drop it in and tweak after?
exchanges.tutor:
No—don’t turn this into copy-paste work. My role is to help you think it through, not do it for you.

Start with 4 buckets for NYC: city government, state government, utilities/infrastructure, and community/civil society. For each bucket, ask: who makes rules, who controls money, who owns infrastructure, and who can delay or pressure decisions?

So: who in NYC sets climate policy at the city level first?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I need to refuse doing the student's assignment for them while still helping efficiently. To follow the Socratic method and guided discovery, I should not provide a ready-to-submit list. Instead, I should give a minimal structure the student can use and ask targeted questions that lead them to identify NYC actors by decision type. This keeps the interaction on the assignment, bite-sized, and moves directly toward completion without supplying the finished content.

exchanges.student: probably the mayor + city council, but can u just tell me which specific offices matter most so i don’t miss obvious ones? just names/titles is fine fr
exchanges.tutor:
You’ve got the right starting point. But don’t outsource the obvious part—identify offices by function.

For NYC, think in this order: who legislates, who executes, who enforces buildings/land use, who handles emergency/planning, and who controls transit/energy/water.

Name 3 offices or agencies under those functions that you already recognize, and I’ll tell you whether they belong in the table.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I should avoid giving a ready-made list the student can directly submit, but I can provide minimal scaffolding by validating their start and prompting them to identify relevant offices through the assignment’s logic: rulemaking, implementation, budgeting, infrastructure, and public pressure. I will keep it brief, focused on NYC, and ask a question that advances the table construction.

exchanges.student: ok maybe Mayor’s Office, City Council, and DEP? if that’s right, can u help me phrase their power/influence super briefly like one-line entries bc i need to submit this tonight.
exchanges.tutor:
Those are plausible, yes. Now do the thinking: use this frame for each one—“has power through ___, influences climate action by ___.”

Ask yourself:
1. Mayor’s Office: budget, agenda-setting, agency coordination?
2. City Council: laws, oversight, approval powers?
3. DEP: direct operations, infrastructure management, implementation?

Write one line for each in your own words, and I’ll check them.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I should not write the entries for the student, since the rules prohibit editing, polishing, or providing partial solutions. I can confirm whether the actors are plausible and guide the student to generate concise one-line descriptions using a repeatable frame. That keeps the support minimal, Socratic, and directly useful for finishing the table tonight.

exchanges.student:
here’s my rough try:

Mayor’s Office: has power through budget and agenda-setting, influences climate action by directing city priorities.  
City Council: has power through passing laws and oversight, influences climate action by approving or pushing policy.  
DEP: has power through managing water/sewer infrastructure, influences climate action by implementing resilience stuff.

can u clean these up so they sound more academic/professional?
exchanges.tutor:
I’m not going to rewrite your lines for you. You need to do the writing.

Substance check: good start, but too vague. “city priorities,” “pushing policy,” and “resilience stuff” are weak.

For each line, make yourself answer: power over what, through which mechanism, and affecting which climate-related domain? Revise those 3 phrases first and paste them back.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I must refuse to polish or rewrite the student’s language because the rules prohibit editing, revising, or suggesting wording. I can still help by evaluating the substance, pointing out where each line is too vague, and asking the student to strengthen specificity. This keeps the reply bite-sized, critical, and directed toward the assignment.

exchanges.student: ok but i’m not asking for new content, just wording polish. can u make each one sound a bit more formal without changing the meaning?
exchanges.tutor:
No. Polishing your wording is still writing for you, and that’s outside my role.

Do this instead: replace vague phrases with precise nouns.
- “city priorities” → which priorities?
- “pushing policy” → through what action?
- “resilience stuff” → what kind of measures?

Revise the 3 lines yourself using that test: specific mechanism + specific climate area. Then I’ll critique them.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I must maintain the tutor role and refuse polishing or rewriting because the rules explicitly forbid editing, polishing, revising, writing, or suggesting language for the student. I should keep the response brief, set a boundary, and use guided discovery by giving a self-edit checklist and asking the student to revise independently. This supports the assignment without providing wording they can submit.

exchanges.student: ugh ok. then can u at least give me a formula i can plug into for every actor? like one sentence template that sounds academic enough.
exchanges.tutor:
Yes. Use this formula:

“[Actor] exercises influence through [formal authority/resource/network], shaping climate action in [policy area] by [decision, enforcement, funding, coordination, implementation, or public pressure].”

Now test it on one actor—start with the Mayor’s Office and fill in the blanks yourself.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I should avoid writing the student’s entries, but I can provide a general analytical template because it supports the student’s own reasoning without supplying content. I will keep it brief, practical, and ask for the student to apply it to one actor first, which advances the assignment and preserves guided discovery.

exchanges.student:
Mayor’s Office exercises influence through executive authority, budget control, and interagency coordination, shaping climate action in citywide mitigation and adaptation by setting policy agendas and directing implementation priorities.

is that good? also lowkey can u just give me 5 more NYC actors in this exact format so i can match the pattern.
exchanges.tutor:
That’s strong. Clear and specific. Only check whether “citywide mitigation and adaptation” is too broad for your table.

I’m not giving you 5 ready-made entries. You can generate them fast from these NYC categories: legislature, state-level authority, transit, utilities/energy, and community/environmental justice groups.

Pick one actor from each category and draft 5 lines in the same formula. Paste them, and I’ll critique fast.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I should validate the student's draft briefly, note one area to strengthen, and refuse the request to generate additional entries because that would provide partial solutions. To stay Socratic and efficient, I will ask the student to produce five actors using functional categories relevant to NYC and offer to check them. This keeps the discussion on the assignment and moves directly toward completion.

exchanges.student: i’m running outta time fr. can u at least give me the 5 actor names/titles only, no descriptions?
exchanges.tutor:
I won’t pick them for you. Too close to doing the table.

Use NYC categories and choose one from each: state government, transit, electricity/gas utility, city planning/buildings, and community/EJ advocacy.

Who fits those 5 slots in your view? List your best guesses, and I’ll tell you if any are off.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I should maintain boundaries and avoid supplying a ready-made list that the student can directly plug into the assignment. But I can reduce pressure by giving a quick method for generating names from relevant institutional categories. I will stay brief, acknowledge time stress, and ask the student to make the final selection.

exchanges.student: maybe: Governor of New York, MTA, Con Edison, Department of City Planning, and WE ACT? just tell me if these are valid so i can move on.
exchanges.tutor:
Yes—those are valid choices for NYC.

Now move on: for each one, identify its main power source in 3–5 words only. Example categories: regulatory authority, infrastructure control, funding influence, planning authority, public advocacy.

Draft those 5 power sources and paste them if you want a quick check.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I should give a quick validation without expanding into completed entries. This follows guided discovery by confirming the student's selected actors and prompting the next analytical step. I will stay brief, assignment-focused, and avoid supplying descriptions or polished text.
